content.text: A service principal is the identity of an application or automated process within a Microsoft Entra ID tenant. Every time an application needs to authenticate and call an API, access an Azure resource, or receive a role assignment, it does so through its service principal — not through a user account.

attrs.alt: App Registration (global definition) instantiates one Service Principal per tenant

content.text: App Registration vs Service Principal

These two objects are closely related but distinct. An App Registration is the blueprint — defined once in the home tenant. A Service Principal is the instantiation of that blueprint in a specific tenant. When a multi-tenant application is consented to in a customer tenant, a Service Principal is automatically created in that customer's tenant.
Aspect
App Registration
Service Principal
Lives in
Home (creator's) tenant only
Every tenant where the app is consented
Identified by
appId (client_id) — same everywhere
object id — unique per tenant
Stores
Redirect URIs, scopes exposed, app roles, credentials
Role assignments, consent grants, app role assignments, tags
Deletable independently
Yes — cascades to home-tenant SP
Yes — removes access in that tenant only
Required for
Defining the application's identity contract
Granting the app permissions in a specific tenant
Always use the service principal's object id (not the appId) when assigning Azure RBAC roles — the appId is the same across tenants but the object id is tenant-specific.
Creating a Service Principal
With Client Secret (CLI)

import subprocess, json

# Create a service principal with a client secret
result = subprocess.run([
    "az", "ad", "sp", "create-for-rbac",
    "--name", "sp-myapp-prod",
    "--role", "Contributor",
    "--scopes", "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-production",
    "--years", "1"
], capture_output=True, text=True)

creds = json.loads(result.stdout)
print("App (client) ID:", creds["appId"])
print("Client secret  :", creds["password"])   # shown only once
print("Tenant ID      :", creds["tenant"])

# Store secret in Key Vault immediately — never in source code
import subprocess
subprocess.run([
    "az", "keyvault", "secret", "set",
    "--vault-name", "kv-myapp-prod",
    "--name", "sp-myapp-client-secret",
    "--value", creds["password"]
])
With Certificate Credentials (preferred for production)

import subprocess, os

# Generate a self-signed certificate (production: use a CA-signed cert)
subprocess.run([
    "openssl", "req", "-x509", "-newkey", "rsa:4096",
    "-keyout", "sp-key.pem", "-out", "sp-cert.pem",
    "-days", "365", "-nodes",
    "-subj", "/CN=sp-myapp-prod"
])

# Upload the certificate to the app registration
APP_ID = "<app-id>"
subprocess.run([
    "az", "ad", "app", "credential", "reset",
    "--id", APP_ID,
    "--cert", "@sp-cert.pem",
    "--append"
])

# Authenticate using the certificate (no password in code)
from azure.identity import CertificateCredential
credential = CertificateCredential(
    tenant_id="<tenant-id>",
    client_id=APP_ID,
    certificate_path="sp-key.pem"   # private key file
)

Certificates are preferred over client secrets because: they cannot be accidentally logged, they never appear in plain text, they are bound to the private key (which never leaves your secure store), and many compliance frameworks require certificate-based credentials for non-human identities.
Authenticating as a Service Principal
Client Credentials Flow

import requests

# Client credentials flow: app authenticates as itself (no user)
TENANT_ID = "<tenant-id>"
CLIENT_ID = "<app-client-id>"
CLIENT_SECRET = "<client-secret>"  # or use certificate credential

resp = requests.post(
    f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token",
    data={
        "grant_type": "client_credentials",
        "client_id": CLIENT_ID,
        "client_secret": CLIENT_SECRET,
        "scope": "https://management.azure.com/.default"
    }
)
token = resp.json()["access_token"]

# Use token to call Azure REST API
rgs = requests.get(
    f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
).json()
for rg in rgs.get("value", []):
    print(rg["name"])
Using the Azure SDK with DefaultAzureCredential

from azure.identity import ClientSecretCredential, CertificateCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient

# Option A: client secret (from Key Vault or env var — never hardcoded)
import os
credential = ClientSecretCredential(
    tenant_id=os.environ["AZURE_TENANT_ID"],
    client_id=os.environ["AZURE_CLIENT_ID"],
    client_secret=os.environ["AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET"]
)

# Option B: certificate (more secure)
credential = CertificateCredential(
    tenant_id=os.environ["AZURE_TENANT_ID"],
    client_id=os.environ["AZURE_CLIENT_ID"],
    certificate_path=os.environ["AZURE_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE_PATH"]
)

rmc = ResourceManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")
for rg in rmc.resource_groups.list():
    print(rg.name)
Service Principal Types
Not all service principals are application-based. Entra ID distinguishes three types:
Type
servicePrincipalType value
Created by
Example
Application
Application
Admin registers app / az ad sp create-for-rbac
sp-myapp-prod
Managed Identity
ManagedIdentity
Azure platform when MI is enabled on a resource
vm-prod (system-assigned MI)
Legacy
Legacy
Pre-2018 tooling (ADAL era)
Older Azure CLI or SDK versions
Managing Service Principals via Microsoft Graph
Listing and Finding Orphaned Service Principals

import requests

BASE = "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0"
HDR  = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"}

# List all service principals (paginated)
url = f"{BASE}/servicePrincipals"
params = {"$select": "id,displayName,appId,servicePrincipalType,createdDateTime", "$top": 999}
sps = []
while url:
    r = requests.get(url, headers=HDR, params=params)
    data = r.json()
    sps.extend(data.get("value", []))
    url = data.get("@odata.nextLink"); params = {}

# Filter: only Application type (not ManagedIdentity or Legacy)
app_sps = [s for s in sps if s.get("servicePrincipalType") == "Application"]
print(f"{len(app_sps)} application service principals")

# Find SPs with no owners (orphaned)
orphaned = []
for sp in app_sps[:50]:  # check first 50 for demo
    owners = requests.get(
        f"{BASE}/servicePrincipals/{sp['id']}/owners",
        headers=HDR
    ).json().get("value", [])
    if not owners:
        orphaned.append(sp["displayName"])

print(f"\nOrphaned (no owner): {len(orphaned)}")
for name in orphaned[:10]:
    print(f"  {name}")
Auditing Expiring Credentials
Assigning Roles to Service Principals

# Assign Reader on a resource group to a service principal
SP_OBJECT_ID=$(az ad sp show --id "<app-id>" --query id -o tsv)

az role assignment create     --assignee-object-id "$SP_OBJECT_ID"     --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal     --role "Reader"     --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod"

# List all role assignments for a service principal (across subscriptions)
az role assignment list     --assignee "$SP_OBJECT_ID"     --all     --query "[].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope}"     -o table

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.authorization import AuthorizationManagementClient
import uuid

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
auth_client = AuthorizationManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")

READER_ROLE = "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7"
SP_OBJECT_ID = "<sp-object-id>"

assignment = auth_client.role_assignments.create(
    scope="/subscriptions/<sub-id>",
    role_assignment_name=str(uuid.uuid4()),
    parameters={
        "properties": {
            "role_definition_id": READER_ROLE,
            "principal_id": SP_OBJECT_ID,
            "principal_type": "ServicePrincipal"
        }
    }
)
print("Assignment:", assignment.id)
Credential Rotation Best Practices
Prefer Managed Identities over service principals with secrets — no credentials to rotate
For service principals that must use credentials, use certificates over client secrets
Set credential expiry to 1 year maximum; automate renewal alerts at 30 days
Store secrets in Azure Key Vault; retrieve at runtime via Managed Identity — never embed in code or CI/CD variables
Use the secret audit script above to find expiring credentials before they cause production outages
Delete old credentials from App Registrations after rotation — expired credentials still appear in audits and cause confusion
Common Mistakes
Mistake
Impact
Fix
Using appId (client_id) in RBAC assignments
Assignment fails silently or hits wrong object
Use the service principal's object id (az ad sp show --id <appId> --query id)
Granting Owner or Contributor at subscription scope
Over-privileged service account; blast radius if compromised
Apply least-privilege: grant only the specific role at the specific resource scope
Client secrets stored in environment variables in CI/CD
Secret exposed in build logs, container images, or IaC state
Use Managed Identities for CI/CD runners (GitHub OIDC, Azure DevOps MI)
Never rotating secrets
Compliance violation; if secret leaks it remains valid indefinitely
Set 1-year expiry; alert at 30 days; automate rotation with Key Vault rotation policies
Deleting the app registration to 'remove' a service principal
Deletes the registration in the home tenant AND all service principals in every tenant
Delete the service principal in the specific tenant, not the app registration, unless fully decommissioning
Federated Identity Credentials (Workload Identity Federation)

Federated credentials let external OIDC-compliant identity providers (GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps, Kubernetes workload identity, Google Cloud) authenticate as your service principal without storing any secret. The external provider issues a short-lived JWT; Entra ID exchanges it for an Azure access token.
This is the recommended authentication pattern for CI/CD pipelines — no secrets to rotate, no credential exposure risk in build logs.
Adding a GitHub Actions Federated Credential

# Federated credential allows GitHub Actions / other OIDC providers
# to authenticate as your SP without storing any secret at all

APP_ID="<app-client-id>"

# Add GitHub Actions federated credential to the app registration
az ad app federated-credential create     --id "$APP_ID"     --parameters '{
        "name": "github-actions-prod",
        "issuer": "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com",
        "subject": "repo:myorg/myrepo:environment:production",
        "description": "GitHub Actions production deployment",
        "audiences": ["api://AzureADTokenExchange"]
    }'

# In GitHub Actions workflow — NO secrets stored in repo:
# jobs:
#   deploy:
#     permissions:
#       id-token: write   # required for OIDC
#       contents: read
#     steps:
#       - uses: azure/login@v2
#         with:
#           client-id: ${{ vars.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
#           tenant-id: ${{ vars.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
#           subscription-id: ${{ vars.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
#       - run: az resource list -g rg-prod -o table
App Roles: API Permission Definitions

App roles let your API define named permission levels that other apps (or users) can be assigned. Unlike OAuth scopes (which are delegated — they act on behalf of a user), app roles can be assigned to applications directly, enabling daemon processes to call your API with specific permissions independent of any user identity.
Defining and Granting App Roles

import requests

APP_ID = "<app-registration-object-id>"   # NOT the appId (client ID)

# Define app roles on the app registration
patch = {
    "appRoles": [
        {
            "id": "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111",  # stable GUID you generate
            "allowedMemberTypes": ["User", "Application"],
            "displayName": "Reports.Read",
            "description": "Can read financial reports",
            "value": "Reports.Read",
            "isEnabled": True
        },
        {
            "id": "22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222222",
            "allowedMemberTypes": ["Application"],
            "displayName": "Reports.Write",
            "description": "Can create and update reports (daemon/service use only)",
            "value": "Reports.Write",
            "isEnabled": True
        }
    ]
}

requests.patch(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/applications/{APP_ID}",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=patch
)

# Grant the Reports.Write role to another service principal (daemon app)
CALLER_SP_ID = "<caller-service-principal-object-id>"
MY_SP_ID     = "<this-app-service-principal-object-id>"
ROLE_ID      = "22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222222"

requests.post(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals/{MY_SP_ID}/appRoleAssignedTo",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={
        "principalId": CALLER_SP_ID,
        "resourceId": MY_SP_ID,
        "appRoleId": ROLE_ID
    }
)

The role value (e.g. 'Reports.Write') appears in the roles claim of the access token. Your API validates the token and checks roles claim to enforce authorisation — this is entirely independent of Azure RBAC.
Consent and Permission Grants

Every OAuth scope or app role a service principal uses is recorded as a consent grant. Reviewing and revoking consent grants is an important security practice — a compromised or over-permissioned app can be de-authorised by deleting its grants.
Inspecting and Revoking Consent Grants
Multi-Tenant Applications

When signInAudience is AzureADMultipleOrgs or AzureADandPersonalMicrosoftAccount, any Entra ID tenant can consent to your application. The app registration stays in your home tenant; a service principal is created in each customer tenant at consent time.
Admin Consent Flow for Multi-Tenant Apps

import requests

# Multi-tenant apps require admin consent in each customer tenant
# The admin consent URL for a specific tenant:
TENANT_ID = "<customer-tenant-id>"
CLIENT_ID = "<your-app-client-id>"
REDIRECT  = "https://myapp.example.com/auth/callback"

consent_url = (
    f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/adminconsent"
    f"?client_id={CLIENT_ID}"
    f"&redirect_uri={REDIRECT}"
    f"&state=<csrf-token>"
)
print("Admin consent URL:", consent_url)
# Redirect the customer's Global Admin to this URL
# After consent: a service principal is created in their tenant automatically

# Check if consent was granted (SP exists in customer tenant)
def check_sp_exists(token: str, app_id: str) -> bool:
    r = requests.get(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"},
        params={"$filter": f"appId eq '{app_id}'", "$select": "id,displayName"}
    )
    return len(r.json().get("value", [])) > 0

print("SP exists in customer tenant:", check_sp_exists(customer_token, CLIENT_ID))
Consent Type
When Used
Who Approves
User consent
App requests low-privilege delegated scopes (User.Read, email, openid)
The individual user at first sign-in
Admin consent (tenant-wide)
App requests high-privilege scopes or app roles, or tenant blocks user consent
Global Admin, Cloud App Admin, or Application Admin
Pre-consent (admin on behalf of all users)
Enterprise app — users never see consent prompt
Admin clicks 'Grant admin consent' in Entra admin center
Service Principal Lifecycle Automation

Large tenants accumulate dozens or hundreds of service principals. Automating their lifecycle — alerting on expiring credentials, identifying unused principals, and enforcing naming conventions — prevents sprawl and security gaps.
Weekly Credential Expiry Report Script
Summary

A service principal is the per-tenant instantiation of an application identity. An App Registration defines the application's contract (scopes, roles, credentials) in the home tenant; a Service Principal is created automatically in every tenant where the app is consented or registered.
Use the service principal's object id (not appId) for RBAC assignments
Three types: Application (your apps), ManagedIdentity (Azure-platform provisioned), Legacy (old tooling)
Authenticate with client credentials flow (secret or certificate); prefer certificate over secret
Prefer Managed Identity over service principals wherever the workload runs on Azure — no credentials at all
Audit expiring credentials with the Graph API; automate renewal alerts at 30 days before expiry
Apply least-privilege: the minimum role at the narrowest scope the workload actually needs
